text: In these exciting times of machine learning (ML) being applied to a wide array of scientific and industrial applications, there is a pressing need for validating and comparing the performance of ML approaches. Choosing the right performance measures and applying the right statistical tools to assess their performance is not straightforward for many researchers. In this review paper, we provide an overview of the most commonly used performance measures appropriate to the different supervised learning setups: - Binary, multi-class and multi-label classification - Regression - Image segmentation - Object detection - Information retrieval We also highlight the importance of statistical hypothesis testing in ascertaining whether the differences in performance estimates of the models being compared are statistically significant or not. We caution the misuse of the paired t-test and advocate for the use of non-parametric tests such as the Wilcoxon signed-rank, Friedman, McNemar and DeLong tests, especially when validating performances on resampled datasets or data samples with non-Gaussian distributions. To illustrate the usefulness of the above performance measures and statistical tests, we present several experimental examples of applications of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to medical imaging where the classification of lung infections from X-ray images and the segmentation of different types of cancers from positron emission tomography (PET) images are considered. This review aims to serve as a reference guide in assessing the validity of different ML approaches.
